theme.name: Idioms
theme.tagline: 80+ PSLE English idioms with frequency tags — drilled via SM-2 spaced repetition

idioms.title: Idioms (Expanded)
idioms.blurb: 80 idioms across fear, joy, sadness, anger, surprise, perseverance, friendship, time. Frequency-tagged from the curated KB.
keyQuestions: How often does this idiom appear in PSLE writing or comprehension?
Is this idiom right for composition, oral, or both?
Have I deployed at most 1-2 idioms in my Continuous Writing piece?

glossary.term: break out in a cold sweat
glossary.definition: To suddenly feel afraid; to begin sweating from fear or shock.
glossary.template: Composition — fear / sudden bad news. Common.

glossary.definition: To feel nervous, especially before something important.
glossary.template: Composition + SBC — nervousness before a performance or exam. Common.

glossary.definition: To be extremely anxious or jumpy.
glossary.template: Composition — anxiety before a big event. Common.

glossary.definition: Terrified to the point of being unable to move.

glossary.term: like a cat on hot bricks
glossary.definition: Restless or jumpy with anxiety; unable to sit still.
Trembling visibly from fear or cold.
Terrified at a sudden moment; gripped by sudden fear.
Unable to move because of fear.
Very pale because of shock or fear.
glossary.template: Composition — describing reaction to bad news. Common.

glossary.definition: Tense and anxious; nervously waiting.
Failed to act because of sudden fear.
Extremely happy; blissful.
glossary.template: Composition + SBC — receiving good news. Very common.

glossary.definition: Blissfully happy; in a state of joy.
Delighted; very pleased.

glossary.term: like a dog with two tails
glossary.definition: Extremely pleased; openly joyful.
Feeling euphoric; floating with happiness.
Extremely happy and confident.
Felt a sudden surge of joy or excitement.
Relief after pressure or worry has lifted.
To feel relief after worry.
Past the most dangerous or difficult part.
To feel grateful for what one has.
Something wished for that has happened.
Choked up with sadness or strong emotion.
Feeling low or depressed.
Felt sudden sadness or disappointment.
With sadness or reluctance.
Greatly diminished from a previous state.
To accept something unpleasant that cannot be avoided.
To regret bitterly.
To disappoint someone.
To admit one was wrong.
To accept the consequences of one's actions.
To meet a challenge well; to perform above expectations.
Great composure under pressure; unwavering courage.
To act bravely or with dignity in difficulty.

glossary.term: between a rock and a hard place
glossary.definition: Facing two equally bad options.
At a point of decision; facing an important choice.
An unfortunate event that turns out to have a hidden good.
Great effort and hard work.
A small visible part of a much larger problem.
To start again from scratch after a plan fails.

glossary.term: bite off more than (you) can chew
glossary.definition: To take on more than one can handle.
A difficult, ongoing struggle.
Repeated practice leads to skill mastery.
To stop a bad habit.
Long-standing habits are difficult to break.
To begin again with a new attitude.
To start behaving better; to reform.

glossary.term: a leopard cannot change its spots
glossary.definition: A person's character is fixed and unlikely to change.
A complete surprise; unexpected news.
Unexpectedly; without warning.
Surprised; unprepared for what happened.

glossary.term: the cat is out of the bag
glossary.definition: A secret is now known.
To have a secret plan ready.

glossary.term: let the cat out of the bag
glossary.definition: To reveal a secret accidentally.
To show one's emotions openly.
To tell the truth; to confess.
A very kind nature.
To make extra effort.
To offer help.
Someone who listens with sympathy.
Time passes quickly.
Just in time; at the last critical moment.
At the last possible moment.

glossary.term: strike while the iron is hot
glossary.definition: To act on an opportunity quickly.
A great chance.
Very rarely.
To learn from a painful experience.
Something understood after a mistake.

glossary.term: don't count (your) chickens before they hatch
glossary.definition: Don't assume success too early.

glossary.term: the early bird catches the worm
glossary.definition: Being early brings rewards.

glossary.term: slow and steady wins the race
glossary.definition: Careful, steady effort beats hurried effort.

glossary.term: two heads are better than one
glossary.definition: Cooperation leads to better outcomes.
To work late into the night, especially studying.
To improve one's effort.
To give one's best effort.
To try very hard to help.
To search or try every option.

glossary.term: a friend in need is a friend indeed
glossary.definition: True friends help during difficult times.
To agree with someone.
To end an argument and make peace.
To start a conversation in an awkward situation.

drillItems.prompt: Pick the idiom that fits: 'After failing twice, he was about to give up — but then he decided to ___ and try one more time.'
options: let the cat out of the bag
drillItems.explanation: 'Bite the bullet' = accept a hard thing bravely. 'Hit the books' = study hard; the other two = reveal a secret.

drillItems.prompt: What does 'a blessing in disguise' mean?
options: Something that looks bad but turns out good.
Something that looks good but is bad.
drillItems.explanation: It looks bad first; the goodness reveals itself later. Useful for R.E.F. endings about setbacks that taught you something.

drillItems.prompt: Which idiom is OVER-USED at PSLE and should be avoided?
options: his eyes lit up like fireworks
her shoulders sagged under the weight
drillItems.explanation: 'Raining cats and dogs' is the textbook cliché. Examiners groan. The other three are fresher specific images.

drillItems.prompt: Best use of 'go the extra mile' in a composition:
options: He went the extra mile and walked home.
She went the extra mile to help her friend, staying back two hours to revise together.
Going the extra mile is good.
I went the extra mile for one mile.
drillItems.explanation: Idioms work when ANCHORED to a specific action. Option B shows what 'extra mile' actually looks like.
= euphoric. Useful for happy climaxes — better than 'I was very happy'.

drillItems.prompt: Which idiom means 'reveal a secret accidentally'?
drillItems.explanation: 'Spill the beans' = let a secret out. 'Break the ice' = ease tension; the other two = study late / die.

drillItems.prompt: 'Hit the nail on the head' means:
options: To injure yourself with a hammer.
To say exactly the right thing.
drillItems.explanation: = got it precisely right. Useful in dialogue: 'You've hit the nail on the head,' she nodded.

drillItems.prompt: How many idioms should you use in a 200-word composition?
drillItems.explanation: 1–2 well-placed idioms beat 6 forced ones. Idioms reward when natural; punish when shoe-horned.

drillItems.prompt: Which idiom means 'two things happen at the same time'?
options: kill two birds with one stone
two heads are better than one
drillItems.explanation: = achieve two things with one action. Different from 'two peas in a pod' (similar) and 'in the same boat' (sharing a situation).

drillItems.prompt: Pick the idiom that fits: 'My grandfather is 90 but he's still ___, going on long walks every morning.'
drillItems.explanation: 'As fit as a fiddle' = very healthy. 'Under the weather' = sick (opposite). The other two = unexpected / very happy.
= very easy. Useful for downplaying a challenge in dialogue.

drillItems.prompt: Best register for: 'My uncle kicked the bucket last week.'
options: Friendly story — fine for an informal note among friends.
Avoid in formal writing AND in a respectful context — it's slang for 'died'.
Use it in a formal letter.
drillItems.explanation: Idioms have register. 'Kicked the bucket' is too casual for a respectful topic about death. Choose neutral words ('passed away') or contextual showing.
